preFix.title: Fairy cakes
preFix.subtitle: Small British iced sponge cakes
preFix.excerpt: Fairy cakes are small, individually baked Victoria sponges iced with a simple glace icing. The batter is an all-in-one sponge: butter, sugar, eggs, and flour beaten together in one bowl. Ready in 30 minutes from bowl to decorated. The classic British version has a flat top iced in pastel colours, not the mountainous frosting of a cupcake.
preFix.sourceNotes: Small iced sponge cakes appear in British domestic cookery books from the Victorian era. Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, includes recipes for small sponge cakes iced with fondant. The name 'fairy cake' distinguishes the flat-iced British version from the deep-frosted American cupcake, which became a separate thing in the late twentieth century. The all-in-one method dates to the mid-twentieth century and the introduction of soft-spreading margarine.

content.text: The all-in-one method puts everything in the bowl at once, which makes this one of the most forgiving cake batters there is. The only requirement is that the butter is properly soft, not just cool-room-temperature firm: press a finger in and it should yield without resistance. If in doubt, cut the butter into small pieces and leave it at room temperature for an hour before you start.

Heat the oven to 180 degrees C fan. Line a 12-hole bun tin with paper cases. Put 125 g softened butter, 125 g caster sugar, 2 large eggs, 125 g self-raising flour, 1 tsp vanilla extract, and 1 tablespoon of milk into a large bowl. Beat with a hand mixer for 1 to 2 minutes until smooth and combined. Divide evenly between the cases; they should be about half full. Bake for 13 to 16 minutes until risen and springy to the touch and a skewer comes out clean. Leave in the tin for 5 minutes, then transfer to a rack and cool completely before icing.

Sift 150 g icing sugar into a bowl. Add 2 tablespoons of boiling water and stir to a smooth, thick paste: it should coat the back of a spoon and slowly find its own level when dropped. Add food colouring if using. Spoon a teaspoon of icing onto each cake and leave to set for 20 minutes. Decorate with sprinkles or hundreds and thousands while the icing is still wet.

items.problem: Cakes have a peak in the middle and a dense crumb
items.solution: The batter was overmixed or the oven too hot. Mix until just combined and check the temperature: 180 degrees C fan is correct. A slight dome is normal; a sharp peak with a tight crumb suggests overworked batter.

items.problem: Icing is too runny and slides off
items.solution: Too much water. Add boiling water a teaspoon at a time; the icing should be thick enough to hold its shape on the spoon. If it has already gone on the cakes, leave them in a cool room for 30 minutes and it will usually firm up.
